original_reasoning: Arabic القسطناس: a type of wood (specifically noted as a hard/strong wood). Greek κάστανα = sweet chestnuts (Castanea sativa). The Arabic is almost certainly a loanword from Greek κάστανον (chestnut) or shares the same Mediterranean source — both refer to the chestnut tree and its material.

Arabic القنطريس: a plant name (cross-reference to قطرس). The plant قنطوريون (centaury/كنتوريون) is a known Arabic medicinal plant name borrowed from Greek κενταύριον (centaury — the plant Centaurium erythraea, named after the centaur Chiron who used it medicinally). The Arabic القنطريس likely refers to the same or related plant via the Κένταυρος mythological connection.

Arabic الكرناس: a variant of الكرباس (fine linen cloth) OR a nose/horn-shaped protrusion. Greek κορωνόπους = buck's horn plantain — named for its horn/beak-shaped leaf lobes. If Arabic sense is 'protrusion', both share the horn-shaped projection imagery.

Arabic الكستيج: a thick cord/rope that Zoroastrians (Magians) tie around their waist — a ritual belt/girdle covering the body. Greek κατάστεγος = covered in/roofed over. Both involve the concept of 'covering/enclosing' — the girdle covers the body, the roof covers a building. Plausible conceptual link, though domains differ.
Arabic خوف: fear/dread. Greek ἐκφεύγω: to flee/escape. Fear and flight are cognitively coupled; plausible semantic overlap.
Arabic خيف: fear/dread. Greek ἐκφεύγω: to flee/escape. Fear and flight are cognitively coupled; plausible semantic overlap.
هندكي (Indian person) ↔ Latin hindicus: same ethnonym
Arabic اصطبل < Latin stabulum (stable for animals); stabilio=to stabilize/make firm — same borrowed root, verb form
